/wiki/Wikipedia:Protection_policy#semi

 From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

/wiki/File:Burj_Khalifa.jpgThe 828-metre-tall (2,717 ft) [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) has been the tallest building since 2009.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The Burj Khalifa has been classified as [megatall](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers).[[2]](#cite_note-CTBUH-2)
/wiki/File:Tallest_buildings_2022.pngA diagram showing the tallest buildings as of 2024
This is a list of the tallest [buildings](/wiki/Building). Tall buildings, such as [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper), are intended here as enclosed structures with continuously occupiable floors and a height of at least 350 metres (1,150 ft). Such definition excludes [non-building structures](/wiki/Non-building_structure), such as [towers](/wiki/Tower).

## History

Historically, the world's tallest man-made structure was the [Great Pyramid of Giza](/wiki/Great_Pyramid_of_Giza) in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt), which held the position for over 3,800 years[[3]](#cite_note-3) until the construction of [Lincoln Cathedral](/wiki/Lincoln_Cathedral) in 1311. The [Strasbourg Cathedral](/wiki/Strasbourg_Cathedral) in [France](/wiki/France), completed in 1439, was the world's tallest building until 1874.

The first building considered to be a skyscraper was the 138 ft (42.1 m) [Home Insurance Building](/wiki/Home_Insurance_Building), built in Chicago in 1885. The [United States](/wiki/United_States) would remain the location of the world's tallest building throughout the 20th century until 1998, when the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) were completed. Since then, two other buildings have gained the title: [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101) in 2004 and [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in 2009.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Since the beginning of the 21st century, the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East), [China](/wiki/China), and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia) have experienced booms in skyscraper construction.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Ranking criteria and alternatives

The international non-profit organization [Council on Vertical Urbanism](/wiki/Council_on_Vertical_Urbanism) (CVU) was formed in 1969 and announces the title of "The World's Tallest Building" and sets the standards by which buildings are measured. It maintains a list of the 100 tallest completed buildings in the world.[[6]](#cite_note-CTBUH_top_100-6) The organization currently ranks [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) in Dubai as the tallest at 828 m (2,717 ft).[[6]](#cite_note-CTBUH_top_100-6) However, the CVU only recognizes buildings that are complete, and some buildings included within the lists in this article are not considered finished by the CVU.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

In 1996, as a response to the dispute as to whether the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) or the [Sears Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) was taller,[[7]](#cite_note-PetronasSears-7) the council listed and ranked buildings in four categories:[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

- height to structural or architectural top;

- height to highest occupied floor;

- height to top of roof (removed as category in November 2009);[[9]](#cite_note-9) and

- height to top of any part of the building.

All categories measure the building from the level of the lowest significant open-air pedestrian entrance.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

[Spires](/wiki/Spire) are considered integral parts of the architectural design of buildings, changes to which would substantially change the appearance and design of the building, whereas antennas may be added or removed without such consequences. The Petronas Towers, with their spires, are thus ranked higher than the Willis Tower (formerly the Sears Tower) with its antennas, despite the Petronas Towers' lower roofs and lower highest point.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

Until 1996, the world's tallest building was defined by the height to the top of the tallest architectural element, including spires but not antennae.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) In 1930, this definitional argument led to a rivalry between the [Bank of Manhattan Building](/wiki/40_Wall_Street) and the [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building). The Bank of Manhattan Building (i.e. 40 Wall Street) employed only a short spire, was 282.5 m (927 ft) tall, and had a much higher top occupied floor (the second category in the 1996 criteria for tallest building). In contrast, the Chrysler Building employed a very large 38.1 m (125 ft) spire secretly assembled inside the building to claim the title of world's tallest building with a total height of 318.9 m (1,046 ft), although it had a lower top occupied floor and a shorter height when both buildings' spires were excluded.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

Upset by Chrysler's victory, [Shreve & Lamb](/wiki/Shreve,_Lamb_%26_Harmon), the consulting architects of the Bank of Manhattan Building, wrote a newspaper article claiming that their building was actually the tallest, since it contained the world's highest usable floor, at 255 m (837 ft). They pointed out that the observation deck in the Bank of Manhattan Building was nearly 30 m (98 ft) above the top floor in the Chrysler Building, whose surpassing spire was strictly ornamental and inaccessible.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Burj Khalifa currently tops the list regardless of which criterion is applied, though at a much lower margin when measured to highest occupied floor.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Tallest buildings in the world

As of 2026, this list includes all 97 buildings (completed and architecturally [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out)) that reach a height of 350 m (1,150 ft) or more, as assessed by their highest architectural feature. The building is considered as architecturally topped out when it is under construction, structurally topped out, fully clad, and the highest finished architectural elements are in place.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

Of these buildings, almost half are in China. Six of the last seven buildings to have held the record as "[tallest building](/wiki/Skyscraper#History_of_the_tallest_skyscrapers)" are still found in the list, with the exception being [the North Tower](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)) of the original [New York](/wiki/New_York_City) [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) at 417 m (1,368 ft) after its destruction in the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks) of 2001. Had the World Trade Center Twin Towers never been destroyed and [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) never built, they would have ranked 39 and 40 on the list today.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

Clear

Denotes building that is or was once the tallest in the world

## Alternative measurements

Parts of this article (those related to the alternative measurement lists) need to be updated. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. (January 2023)

### Height to pinnacle (highest point)

/wiki/File:Tallest_Buildings_in_the_World_by_pinnacle_height.pngTallest buildings by pinnacle height, including all masts, poles, antennae, etc. in 2014
This measurement disregards distinctions between architectural and non-architectural extensions, and simply measures to the highest point, irrespective of material or function of the highest element.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

This measurement is useful for [air traffic obstacle](/wiki/Air_traffic_obstacle) determinations, and is also a wholly objective measure. However, this measurement includes extensions that are easily added, removed, and modified from a building and are independent of the overall structure.

This measurement only recently came into use, when the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) passed the Sears Tower (now named [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower)) in height. The former was considered taller because its spires were considered architectural, while the latter's antennae were not. This led to the split of definitions, with the Sears Tower claiming the lead in this and the height-to-roof (now highest occupied floor) categories, and with the Petronas claiming the lead in the architectural height category.

If the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) towers were still standing, the [North](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) (1,728 feet (526.7 m), including the antenna) and [South](/wiki/2_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) Towers (1,377 feet (419.7 m)) would fall between numbers 11 and 37 on the current list (as it can be assumed the rebuilt One World Trade Center would have never been built).

Six of the last seven buildings to have held the record as "[tallest building](/wiki/Skyscraper#History_of_the_tallest_skyscrapers)" are still found in the list, with the exception being [the North Tower](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)) of the original [New York](/wiki/New_York_City) [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) at 417 m (1,368 ft) after its destruction in the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks) of 2001. Had the World Trade Center Twin Towers never been destroyed and [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) never built, they would have ranked 39 and 40 on the list today.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

Clear

Denotes building that is or was once the tallest in the world

## Alternative measurements

Parts of this article (those related to the alternative measurement lists) need to be updated. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. (January 2023)

### Height to pinnacle (highest point)

/wiki/File:Tallest_Buildings_in_the_World_by_pinnacle_height.pngTallest buildings by pinnacle height, including all masts, poles, antennae, etc. in 2014
This measurement disregards distinctions between architectural and non-architectural extensions, and simply measures to the highest point, irrespective of material or function of the highest element.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

This measurement is useful for [air traffic obstacle](/wiki/Air_traffic_obstacle) determinations, and is also a wholly objective measure. However, this measurement includes extensions that are easily added, removed, and modified from a building and are independent of the overall structure.

This measurement only recently came into use, when the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers) passed the Sears Tower (now named [Willis Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower)) in height. The former was considered taller because its spires were considered architectural, while the latter's antennae were not. This led to the split of definitions, with the Sears Tower claiming the lead in this and the height-to-roof (now highest occupied floor) categories, and with the Petronas claiming the lead in the architectural height category.

If the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)) towers were still standing, the [North](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) (1,728 feet (526.7 m), including the antenna) and [South](/wiki/2_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001)) Towers (1,377 feet (419.7 m)) would fall between numbers 11 and 37 on the current list (as it can be assumed the rebuilt One World Trade Center would have never been built).

Designed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill), it is the [world's tallest structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_the_world), with a total height of 829.8 m (2,722 ft, or just over half a mile) and a roof height (excluding the antenna, but including a 242.6 m spire)[[2]](#cite_note-Vanity-3) of 828 m (2,717 ft). Construction of the Burj Khalifa began in 2004; the exterior was completed five years later. The primary structure is [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete). Some of the [structural steel](/wiki/Structural_steel) for the building was salvaged from the demolished [Palace of the Republic](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Republic,_Berlin) in East Berlin.[[5]](#cite_note-dw-6) The building was opened in 2010 as part of a new development called [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai). It was designed to be the centerpiece of large-scale, mixed-use development.

The building is named after the former president of the United Arab Emirates (UAE), Sheikh [Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan](/wiki/Khalifa_bin_Zayed_Al_Nahyan).[[6]](#cite_note-USAtoday-7) The [United Arab Emirates government](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_government) provided Dubai with financial support as the developer, [Emaar Properties](/wiki/Emaar_Properties), experienced financial problems during the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession). The Shanghai Tower[[a]](#cite_note-9) is a 128-story, 632-meter-tall (2,073-foot) [megatall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) located in [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui), [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai).[[9]](#cite_note-uc-10) It is currently the [tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China), and the world's [third-tallest building by height to architectural top](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings). It was the tallest and largest [LEED](/wiki/LEED)-CS Platinum certified building in the world from 2015 to 2024. It was also the second tallest-building in the world, from 2015 to 2021, until the completion of [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) in Malaysia. The Shanghai Tower also had the world's fastest [elevators](/wiki/Elevator) at a top speed of 20.5 meters per second (74 kilometers per hour; 46 miles per hour) until 2017,[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) when it was surpassed by the [Guangzhou CTF Finance Center](/wiki/Guangzhou_CTF_Finance_Center), with its top speed of 21 meters per second (76 km/h; 47 mph).[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Designed by the international design firm [Gensler](/wiki/Gensler) and owned by the Shanghai Municipal Government,[[1]](#cite_note-OwnersWSJ-1) it is the tallest of the world's first triple-adjacent supertall buildings in [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong), the other two being the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower) and the [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center). Its tiered construction, designed for high energy efficiency, provides nine separate zones divided between office, retail and leisure use.[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4)[[6]](#cite_note-FT2013N-6)[[13]](#cite_note-:1-14) The US-based [Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat](/wiki/Council_on_Tall_Buildings_and_Urban_Habitat) cites it as "one of the most sustainably advanced tall buildings in the world."[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Groundbreaking and construction work on the tower began on 29 November 2008 and [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out) on 4 August 2013.[[9]](#cite_note-uc-10)[[15]](#cite_note-16) The exterior was completed in summer 2015,[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[13]](#cite_note-:1-14) and work was considered complete in September 2014. Although the building was originally scheduled to open to the public in November 2014, the actual public-use date was shifted to February 2015. The [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) was opened to visitors in July 2016; the period from July through September 2018 was term
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="374c56864d34cfa6">>>"

The Ping An Finance Center ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language): 平安金融中心; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Píng'ān Jīnróng Zhōngxīn) is a 115-[storey](/wiki/Storey), 599.1-metre (1,966-foot) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/Supertall) in [Shenzhen, Guangdong](/wiki/Shenzhen,_Guangdong), China.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was completed in 2017,[[1]](#cite_note-CTBUH-1) and is the [tallest building in Shenzhen](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Shenzhen), the [2nd tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) (after the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower)), and the [5th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_world).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3) The building contains office, hotel and retail spaces, a conference center, and a high-end shopping mall.

The [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) is named Free Sky and is located on floor 116,[[9]](#cite_note-thetowerinfo-9) which broke the record of having the highest observation deck in a building at 562.5 m (1,845 ft).[[10]](#cite_note-ctbuh.org-10) The observation deck allows 360-degree views. Inside there are a transparent viewing area, interactive experience area, and special display area to showcase youth cultures with Shenzhen special characteristics through multimedia facilities. The opening hours are 10am to 8pm, last entry 7.15pm.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The building houses the headquarters of [Ping An Insurance](/wiki/Ping_An_Insurance), with their company name representing the building name. The building was commissioned by Ping An Insurance and designed by the American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates). The design of the building is meant to be unique and elegant, and to represent the history and achievements of the main tenant. A stain

It is [the sixth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), the tallest in [OECD](/wiki/OECD) countries and [in South Korea](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_South_Korea), and also the first in South Korea to be over 100 stories tall. It was the fifth-tallest in the world when completed.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The tower opened to the public on April 3, 2017.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A "Sky Bridge Tour" is located on the roof of Lotte World Tower at 541 m (1,775 ft),[[6]](#cite_note-6) which is about the same height of [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City) (1,776 feet or 541 meters), the [tallest building in the United States](/wiki/Tallest_building_in_the_United_States).

After 13 years of planning and site preparation,[[7]](#cite_note-world.kbs.co.kr-7) the tower gained final approval to start construction by the government in November 2010[[7]](#cite_note-world.kbs.co.kr-7) and the first groundbreaking activities of piling and frame assembly were observed at the construction site in March 2012.

On December 31, 2015, the [LED](/wiki/LED)-[pixels](/wiki/Pixel) of the façade displayed the number "2016".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On April 3, 2017, Lotte shot off fireworks to celebrate the tower's official opening.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Fireworks display of the Lotte World Tower's grand opening
On January 1, 2018, Lotte shot off fireworks with a LED laser show for seven minutes to celebrate New Year's Day and the [2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On March 17, 2016, before the final phase of external construction, the [diagrid](/wiki/Diagrid) lantern-shaped roof structure was completed. The roof structure was constructed with steel counterparts that are each 12 m (39 ft) and weigh 20 tonnes (20 long tons; 22 short tons). At 530 m (1,740 ft), and with 97 floors, the tower is the second-tallest building in Municipal Tianjin after unfinished [Goldin Finance 117](/wiki/Goldin_Finance_117), the [eighth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), and the tallest building in the world with fewer than 100 floors.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is located in the outer district of the [Tianjin Economic-Technological Development Area](/wiki/Tianjin_Economic-Technological_Development_Area). The building was honored with the Tall/Slender Structure award at the 2021 Awards Ceremony by the Institution of Structural Engineers (IStructE).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Tianjin CTF Finance Centre is designed with a gently curving glass facade that incorporates eight sloping mega-columns. The columns follow a line connecting the centers and corners of all four elevations. This design improves the building's seismic resilience and contributes to both its gravity and lateral systems. The columns are effective in increasing the stiffness of the building's perimeter frame, consequently attracting a larger portion of the seismic forces in compliance with the Chinese code requirements.

The façade reinforces the curvature of the tower form and creates a shimmering texture over the building's surface. The crystalline-like curtain wall stretches from the suspended glass canopies at each of the lobbies to the dematerialized, mega-column-looped crown and presents a bold expression of a comprehensive, integrated design on the Tianjin skyline.

/wiki/File:Citic_Tower_Office_Lobby_2014.jpgCITIC Tower Office Lobby
/wiki/File:HK_Queensway_Citic_Tower_Lung_Wui_Road_Blue_Sky_1.JPGCITIC Tower from [Lung Wui Road](/w/index.php?title=Lung_Wui_Road&action=edit&redlink=1)
CITIC Tower ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language): 中信大廈) is a 33-storey [office building](/wiki/Office_building) on [Tim Mei Avenue](/wiki/Tim_Mei_Avenue), [Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty,_Hong_Kong), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong). It is the corporate headquarters of [CITIC Pacific](/wiki/CITIC_Limited) Ltd, a [conglomerate](/wiki/Conglomerate_(company)) publicly traded on the [Hong Kong Stock Exchange](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stock_Exchange) and listed on the [Hang Seng Index](/wiki/Hang_Seng_Index), and also a subsidiary of the [CITIC Group](/wiki/CITIC_Group).

CITIC Tower is also the headquarters of another development partner, [Kerry Group](/wiki/Kerry_Group).

It is one of the participants in the nightly [A Symphony of Lights](/wiki/A_Symphony_of_Lights) (幻彩詠香江) light show on both sides of [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour).

In an [authorised protest in 2019](/wiki/2019%E2%80%932020_Hong_Kong_protests), police surrounded the tower on both sides, trapping protesters and fired tear gas into the crowd of protesters. International experts called the use of tear gas excessive, "actually inciting and causing what looks like a stampede".[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Design and construction
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CITIC_Tower&action=edit&section=1)]

The tower was conceived as an equilateral triangular block with landscaped sky gardens at various levels. The architecture was designed by [P&T Group](/wiki/P%26T_Group). Its unique design is composed of equilateral triangles and circles.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

CITIC Tower was completed in 1997 under a fast track development programme.

## Location and facilities
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CITIC_Tower&action=edit&section=2)]

It borders the [Tamar site](/wiki/Tamar,_Hong_Kong) in Admiralty and features a view of [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour). At the lower levels there is a shopping mall and carpark. There is a footbridge connecting it with [Admiralty Centre](/wiki/Admiralty_Centre) and [MTR](/wiki/MTR) [Admiralty station](/wiki/Admiralty_station_(MTR)).

The [government](/wiki/Government_of_Hong_Kong) headquarters buildings are adjacent to CITIC Tower.

- [Maybank](/wiki/Maybank) (7/F,18/F)

- [CITIC Pacific](/wiki/CITIC_Limited) Ltd (29/F-32/F)

- [CITIC Capital](/wiki/CITIC_Capital) (27/F-28/F)

CITIC Tower ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language): 中信大廈) is a 33-storey [office building](/wiki/Office_building) on [Tim Mei Avenue](/wiki/Tim_Mei_Avenue), [Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty,_Hong_Kong), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong). It is the corporate headquarters of [CITIC Pacific](/wiki/CITIC_Limited) Ltd, a [conglomerate](/wiki/Conglomerate_(company)) publicly traded on the [Hong Kong Stock Exchange](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stock_Exchange) and listed on the [Hang Seng Index](/wiki/Hang_Seng_Index), and also a subsidiary of the [CITIC Group](/wiki/CITIC_Group).

CITIC Tower is also the headquarters of another development partner, [Kerry Group](/wiki/Kerry_Group).

It is one of the participants in the nightly [A Symphony of Lights](/wiki/A_Symphony_of_Lights) (幻彩詠香江) light show on both sides of [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour).

In an [authorised protest in 2019](/wiki/2019%E2%80%932020_Hong_Kong_protests), police surrounded the tower on both sides, trapping protesters and fired tear gas into the crowd of protesters. International experts called the use of tear gas excessive, "actually inciting and causing what looks like a stampede".[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Design and construction
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CITIC_Tower&action=edit&section=1)]

The tower was conceived as an equilateral triangular block with landscaped sky gardens at various levels. The architecture was designed by [P&T Group](/wiki/P%26T_Group). Its unique design is composed of equilateral triangles and circles.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

CITIC Tower was completed in 1997 under a fast track development programme.

## Location and facilities
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CITIC_Tower&action=edit&section=2)]

It borders the [Tamar site](/wiki/Tamar,_Hong_Kong) in Admiralty and features a view of [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour). At the lower levels there is a shopping mall and carpark. There is a footbridge connecting it with [Admiralty Centre](/wiki/Admiralty_Centre) and [MTR](/wiki/MTR) [Admiralty station](/wiki/Admiralty_station_(MTR)).

The [government](/wiki/Government_of_Hong_Kong) headquarters buildings are adjacent to CITIC Tower.

The nickname "China Zun" comes from the [zun](/wiki/Zun), an ancient Chinese wine vessel which inspired the building design, according to the developers, the [CITIC Group](/wiki/CITIC_Group). The groundbreaking ceremony of the building took place in Beijing on 19 September 2011, and the constructors expected to finish the project within five years. CITIC Tower is [Northern China](/wiki/North_China)'s third-tallest building after [Goldin Finance 117](/wiki/Goldin_Finance_117) and [Chow Tai Fook Binhai Center](/wiki/Tianjin_CTF_Finance_Centre) in [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin).
